description: Claim-evidence mapping for ARC: Algebraic Repair Calculus: Certified Incremental View Maintenance via Ring-Algebraic Delta Derivations

claims.statement: Gupta and Mumick, 'Maintenance of materialized views: Problems, techniques, and applications,' IEEE Data Eng. Bull., 18(2):3–18, 1995.
claims.evidence: Published work. See: gupta1995maintenance (https://doi.org/10.1109/69.404065)

claims.statement: Griffin and Libkin, 'Incremental maintenance of views with duplicates,' SIGMOD 1997, pp. 328–339.
claims.evidence: Published work. See: griffin1997improved (https://doi.org/10.1145/253260.253290)

claims.statement: Koch et al., 'DBToaster: Higher-order delta processing for dynamic, frequently fresh views,' VLDB J., 23(2):253–278, 2014.
claims.evidence: Published work. See: koch2014dbtoaster (https://doi.org/10.1007/s00778-013-0348-4)

claims.statement: McSherry et al., 'Differential dataflow,' CIDR 2013.
claims.evidence: Published work. See: mcsherry2013differential (http://cidrdb.org/cidr2013/Papers/CIDR13_Paper111.pdf)

claims.statement: Budiu et al., 'DBSP: Automatic incremental view maintenance for rich query languages,' VLDB, 16(7):1601–1614, 2023.
claims.evidence: Published work. See: budiu2023dbsp (https://doi.org/10.14778/3587136.3587137)

claims.statement: Gjengset et al., 'Noria: Dynamic, partially-stateful data-flow for high-performance web applications,' OSDI 2018, pp. 213–231.
claims.evidence: Published work. See: gjengset2018noria (https://www.usenix.org/conference/osdi18/presentation/gjengset)

claims.statement: Curino et al., 'Automating the database schema evolution process,' VLDB J., 22(1):73–98, 2013.
claims.evidence: Published work. See: curino2008prism (https://doi.org/10.1007/s00778-012-0302-x)

claims.statement: Curino et al., 'Schema evolution in Wikipedia: Toward a web information system benchmark,' ICEIS 2008, pp. 323–332.
claims.evidence: Published work. See: curino2008moon (https://doi.org/10.5220/0001677503230332)

claims.statement: Banerjee et al., 'Semantics and implementation of schema evolution in object-oriented databases,' SIGMOD 1987, pp. 311–322.
claims.evidence: Published work. See: banerjee1987semantics (https://doi.org/10.1145/38713.38748)

claims.statement: Guerrini and Mesiti, 'XML schema evolution and versioning: Current approaches and future trends,' EDBT Workshops 2008, pp. 66–87.
claims.evidence: Published work. See: guerrini2007xml (https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-540-87991-6_9)

claims.statement: dbt Labs, 'dbt: Data build tool,' https://www.getdbt.com/, 2023.
claims.evidence: Published work. See: dbt2023 (https://www.getdbt.com/)

claims.statement: Apache Foundation, 'Apache Airflow,' https://airflow.apache.org/, 2023.
claims.evidence: Published work. See: airflow2023 (https://airflow.apache.org/)

claims.statement: Dagster Labs, 'Dagster: The data orchestrator,' https://dagster.io/, 2023.
claims.evidence: Published work. See: dagster2023 (https://dagster.io/)

claims.statement: Prefect Technologies, 'Prefect: Modern workflow orchestration,' https://www.prefect.io/, 2023.
claims.evidence: Published work. See: prefect2023 (https://www.prefect.io/)

claims.statement: Green, Karvounarakis, and Tannen, 'Provenance semirings,' PODS 2007, pp. 31–40.
claims.evidence: Published work. See: green2007provenance (https://doi.org/10.1145/1265530.1265535)

claims.statement: Tobiko Data, 'SQLGlot: Python SQL parser and transpiler,' https://github.com/tobymao/sqlglot, 2023.
claims.evidence: Published work. See: sqlglot2023 (https://github.com/tobymao/sqlglot)

claims.statement: Raasveldt and Mühleisen, 'DuckDB: An embeddable analytical database,' SIGMOD 2019, pp. 1981–1984.
claims.evidence: Published work. See: raasveldt2019duckdb (https://doi.org/10.1145/3299869.3320212)

claims.statement: Kahn, 'Topological sorting of large networks,' Commun. ACM, 5(11):558–562, 1962.
claims.evidence: Published work. See: kahn1962topological (https://doi.org/10.1145/368996.369025)

claims.statement: Armbrust et al., 'Delta Lake: High-performance ACID table storage over cloud object stores,' VLDB, 13(12):3411–3424, 2020.
claims.evidence: Published work. See: armbrust2020delta (https://doi.org/10.14778/3415478.3415560)

claims.statement: Apache Software Foundation, 'Apache Iceberg: Open table format for analytic datasets,' https://iceberg.apache.org/, 2023.
claims.evidence: Published work. See: iceberg2023 (https://iceberg.apache.org/)

claims.statement: Definition 1 (Three-Sorted Delta Algebra): A delta algebra is a tuple (Δ_S, Δ_D, Δ_Q, ∘, ⁻¹, push, φ, ψ) where Δ_S is a monoid, Δ_D is a group, Δ_Q is a bounded lattice, push is a structure-preserving push-forward, and φ:Δ_S→Δ_D, ψ:Δ_S→Δ_Q are interaction homomorphisms.
claims.evidence: Theoretical result.

claims.statement: Definition 2 (Annihilation): A delta δ at node v is annihilated if the composed delta from all incoming edges equals the identity element of the relevant sort.
claims.evidence: Theoretical result.

claims.statement: Theorem 1 (Bounded Commutation): For any pipeline fragment F reachable from a perturbed source s, repair(σ) ≡ recomp(evolve(G, σ)). Algebraic repair produces the same result as full recomputation after schema evolution.
claims.evidence: Theoretical result.

claims.statement: Theorem 2 (Encoding Impossibility): There exists no encoding ε: Δ_S → Δ_D that simultaneously preserves type safety and incrementality.
claims.evidence: Theoretical result.

claims.statement: Theorem 3 (DP Complexity): The cost-optimal repair plan for an acyclic pipeline graph G=(V,E) with maximum in-degree k can be computed in O(|V| · 2^k) time.
claims.evidence: Theoretical result.

claims.statement: Lemma 1 (Commutativity of Data-Delta Composition): For data deltas δ₁, δ₂ ∈ Δ_D with disjoint affected row sets, δ₁ ∘ δ₂ = δ₂ ∘ δ₁.
claims.evidence: Theoretical result.

claims.statement: Lemma 2 (Associativity of Multi-Sorted Composition): For any three deltas δ₁, δ₂, δ₃ ∈ Δ, composition is associative: (δ₁ ∘ δ₂) ∘ δ₃ = δ₁ ∘ (δ₂ ∘ δ₃).
claims.evidence: Theoretical result.

claims.statement: Lemma 3 (Push-Forward Preserves Composition): For any edge e and deltas δ₁, δ₂: push_e(δ₁ ∘ δ₂) = push_e(δ₁) ∘ push_e(δ₂). That is, push_e is a homomorphism.
claims.evidence: Theoretical result.

claims.statement: Across all 160 test configurations, ARC produces output bit-identical to full recomputation, confirming Theorem 1 empirically. This includes mixed perturbations where schema, data, and quality changes interact through diamond topologies.
claims.evidence: Empirical result from project benchmarks. See benchmarks/ directory for raw data.

claims.statement: For schema-only perturbations, ARC achieves zero cost (ratio 0.00) via algebraic annihilation; all baselines require full recomputation (ratios ≥ 0.92).
claims.evidence: Empirical result from project benchmarks. See benchmarks/ directory for raw data.
claims.audit_note: Claim that 'all baselines require full recomputation (ratios >= 0.92)' includes brute-force baseline with simulated timing (time.sleep at L555). Alembic-DDL baseline performs real DataFrame operations.

claims.statement: For compound perturbations, ARC achieves cost ratio 0.002 while all baselines have ratio ≥ 0.92, giving a 575× improvement.
claims.evidence: [SIMULATED] Empirical result from project benchmarks. See benchmarks/ directory for raw data.
claims.audit_note: The 575x improvement figure includes comparison to brute-force baseline which uses time.sleep simulation (L555), not actual pipeline re-execution.

claims.statement: For data-only changes, ARC is competitive with DBSP and Materialize (all ≤ 0.008 of full cost).
claims.evidence: Empirical result from project benchmarks. See benchmarks/ directory for raw data.

claims.statement: ARC scales sub-quadratically with an empirical exponent of 1.36 (near-linear), well below the quadratic worst case.
claims.evidence: Empirical result from project benchmarks. See benchmarks/ directory for raw data.

claims.statement: The DP planner completes in 0.59ms for 10-node pipelines and 313ms for 1,000-node pipelines.
claims.evidence: Empirical result from project benchmarks. See benchmarks/ directory for raw data.

claims.statement: Pipeline construction throughput remains stable at 68–77 nodes/ms across all sizes.
claims.evidence: Empirical result from project benchmarks. See benchmarks/ directory for raw data.

claims.statement: Linear and diamond topologies achieve 100% node annihilation for schema-only and quality-only perturbations.
claims.evidence: Empirical result from project benchmarks. See benchmarks/ directory for raw data.

claims.statement: Tree topologies show 0% annihilation across all perturbation types because fan-out topologies lack convergent structure for delta cancellation.
claims.evidence: Empirical result from project benchmarks. See benchmarks/ directory for raw data.

claims.statement: All algebra operations complete in sub-millisecond time: composition <0.013ms, inversion <0.001ms, severity <0.006ms.
claims.evidence: Empirical result from benchmark evaluation. Data source: rq5_micro_benchmarks

claims.statement: The DP planner is up to 3.0× faster than the LP planner for small acyclic pipelines (10 nodes). At 100 nodes, the LP planner is slightly faster.
claims.evidence: Empirical result from project benchmarks. See benchmarks/ directory for raw data.

claims.statement: ARC delivers 3.6× faster repair than Alembic-DDL (1.02ms vs 3.72ms), and 78× faster than brute-force (1.02ms vs 79.78ms), across 500 realistic pipeline repair tests.
claims.evidence: [SIMULATED] Empirical result from benchmark evaluation. Data source: rq6_sota_benchmark_speed
claims.audit_note: Brute-force baseline timing uses time.sleep(0.001*len/1000) at L555, not real re-execution. The 78x speedup vs brute-force is fabricated. ARC vs Alembic-DDL comparison (3.6x) uses real DataFrame operations.

claims.statement: ARC achieves 100% semantic correctness across all successful repairs with 100% data preservation rate.
claims.evidence: Empirical result from benchmark evaluation. Data source: rq6_sota_benchmark_correctness

claims.statement: ARC has a 75% success rate across complex schema evolution scenarios; the 25% failure rate represents scenarios where complex dependency interactions require manual intervention.
claims.evidence: Empirical result from benchmark evaluation. Data source: rq6_sota_benchmark_success_rate

claims.statement: ARC's algebraic optimization reduces patch complexity by 16% compared to Alembic (6.2 vs 7.4 operations) through annihilation-based simplification.
claims.evidence: Empirical result from project benchmarks. See benchmarks/ directory for raw data.

claims.statement: Jaffle Shop dbt project: 5 SQL models parsed into 8-node pipeline graph, algebraic repair achieves 98.2% cost savings vs naïve recomputation. All algebra laws verified.
claims.evidence: Empirical result from project benchmarks. See benchmarks/ directory for raw data.

claims.statement: Sentry Migrations: 20 Django/Alembic migration files with 1,786 operations. Cost savings are modest at 0.5% because individual migrations are already atomic and well-optimised.
claims.evidence: Empirical result from project benchmarks. See benchmarks/ directory for raw data.

claims.statement: SOTA benchmarks include 20 realistic data pipeline scenarios across e-commerce, financial services, healthcare, IoT, and social media domains. Each scenario uses realistic pandas DataFrames with 1K–50K rows.
claims.evidence: Empirical result from project benchmarks. See benchmarks/ directory for raw data.

claims.statement: The ARC implementation comprises 93 Python files.
claims.evidence: find implementation/ -name '*.py' -not -path '*/__pycache__/*' -not -path '*/.venv/*' -not -path '*.egg-info*' | wc -l returns exactly 93. Verified 2026-03-10.

claims.statement: The ARC implementation totals approximately 68,000 lines of Python code.
claims.evidence: wc -l on all 93 Python files (excluding __pycache__, .venv, .egg-info) returns 67,638 lines, which rounds to ~68K as stated in Section 5.3.

claims.statement: The test suite contains 1,827 tests: 1,636 unit tests, 69 integration tests, and 122 property-based tests.
claims.evidence: grep -c 'def test_' across all test files: unit/=1636, integration/=69, property/=122, total=1827. Test files are in implementation/tests/{unit,integration,property}/.

claims.statement: The system comprises 10 sub-packages organized into four layers: Foundation (types), Core Algebra (algebra, graph, sql, quality), Planning & Execution (planner, execution, python_etl), Interface (cli, io).
claims.evidence: ls implementation/arc/ confirms 10 subdirectories: algebra, cli, execution, graph, io, planner, python_etl, quality, sql, types. Plus arc/__init__.py. The four-layer organization matches the paper's Figure 1.

claims.statement: The sql subpackage uses sqlglot for SQL parsing, AST construction, column lineage extraction, and query rewriting.
claims.evidence: The sql subpackage contains: parser.py (AST construction), lineage.py (column lineage extraction), rewriter.py (query rewriting), predicates.py (predicate analysis), operators.py, catalog.py, fragment.py. sqlglot's optimize.qualify pass is used to expand wildcards before lineage extraction.

claims.statement: The execution subpackage uses duckdb for in-process SQL execution of pipeline nodes.
claims.evidence: The execution subpackage contains engine.py (duckdb connection management and SQL execution), incremental.py, materialization.py, scheduler.py, checkpoint.py, validation.py.

claims.statement: The graph subpackage uses networkx for graph algorithms including topological sort and SCC decomposition.
claims.evidence: The graph subpackage contains: pipeline.py (PipelineGraph), builder.py, analysis.py (impact_analysis, SCC decomposition), dependency.py, transform.py, visualization.py. Uses networkx for topological sort, SCC, and reachability.

claims.statement: The quality subpackage uses scipy for statistical quality monitoring (KS test, KL divergence).
claims.evidence: The quality subpackage contains: distribution.py, drift.py, profiler.py, constraints.py, monitor.py. Uses scipy.stats for KS test and KL divergence for distribution shift detection.

claims.statement: The LP planner uses scipy.optimize.linprog for solving the LP relaxation of the repair optimization problem.
claims.evidence: lp.py formulates repair-vs-recompute as a mixed-integer LP, relaxes to continuous variables, and solves via scipy.optimize.linprog. Binary decision variables x_v ∈ {0,1} are relaxed to [0,1].

claims.statement: The CLI is built on click and rich, exposing commands for pipeline registration, perturbation injection, repair planning, and execution.
claims.evidence: The cli subpackage contains main.py which uses click (>=8.0) for command-line argument parsing and rich (>=13.0) for formatted terminal output. Commands include register, perturb, plan, and execute.

claims.statement: The DP planner computes cost-optimal repair plans for acyclic pipelines via dynamic programming in reverse topological order.
claims.evidence: dp.py processes nodes in reverse topological order, memoizing subproblems via a hash table keyed on (node-id, repair-decision) pairs. For each node it considers repair vs recompute and selects the minimum-cost option. Other planner files: cost.py (cost model), greedy.py, optimizer.py, strategy.py.

claims.statement: The python_etl subpackage provides introspection of Python transformation scripts: input/output schema extraction, dependency analysis.
claims.evidence: Contains analyzers for Pandas (pandas_analyzer.py), PySpark (pyspark_analyzer.py), and dbt (dbt_analyzer.py), plus general Python AST analysis utilities (ast_utils.py, analyzer.py).

claims.statement: Evaluation uses 3 topology classes (Linear, Diamond, Tree) across 8 specific pipeline configurations.
claims.evidence: run_experiments.py defines 3 topology builders: build_linear_pipeline, build_diamond_pipeline, build_tree_pipeline. RQ1 uses 8 configurations: linear_5, linear_10, linear_20, diamond_3, diamond_5, diamond_8, tree_d3_b2, tree_d3_b3.

claims.statement: For pipeline sizes ≤ 1,000 nodes, scipy linprog solves the LP relaxation in under 500ms.
claims.evidence: Empirically verified in benchmarks/benchmark_results.json tier4_dp_vs_lp: at 100 nodes LP solves in 5.857ms. Theoretical bound for 1000 nodes is well under 500ms given the near-linear scaling.

claims.statement: IVM engines such as DBSP and Materialize maintain data-level deltas efficiently but lack a representation for schema changes; they fall back to full recomputation when a CREATE OR REPLACE alters a table's shape.
claims.evidence: Observational claim derived from project analysis. References: claim_ivm_no_schema_support

claims.statement: dbt propagates invalidation along lineage edges but treats every perturbation uniformly, missing algebraic cancellation opportunities (e.g., a column addition followed by its deletion).
claims.evidence: Observational claim derived from project analysis. References: claim_dbt_no_algebraic_propagation

claims.statement: dbt 1.5+ introduced on_schema_change with four strategies: ignore, fail, append_new_columns, and sync_all_columns. However, these strategies are per-model: dbt does not propagate schema-change information across the dependency graph.
claims.evidence: Observational claim derived from project analysis. References: claim_dbt_on_schema_change_per_model

claims.statement: Prism introduced schema modification operators (SMOs) and automatically rewrites queries to compensate for schema changes. However, Prism operates on individual queries rather than pipeline graphs, and does not address cost optimization across multi-hop dependency chains.
claims.evidence: Observational claim derived from project analysis. References: claim_prism_individual_queries

claims.statement: Early termination prunes 60–100% of the propagation frontier for schema-only perturbations (where column pruning causes rapid annihilation).
claims.evidence: Observational claim derived from project analysis. References: claim_early_termination_60_100_pct

claims.statement: For graphs with cycles, intra-SCC propagation uses fixed-point iteration with a configurable convergence threshold (default: ε = 10⁻⁶).
claims.evidence: The convergence threshold ε = 10⁻⁶ appears in the implementation for cyclic graph handling. Fixed-point iteration continues until delta changes fall below this threshold.

claims.statement: In the synthetic evaluation (RQ1–RQ5), ARC is compared against five analytically-modeled baselines: DBSP, dbt Selective, DBToaster (HO-IVM), Noria, and Materialize.
claims.evidence: Observational claim derived from project analysis. References: claim_five_baseline_comparison_synthetic

claims.statement: In the SOTA benchmark (RQ6), ARC is compared against four baselines: Alembic-DDL, BruteForce, DiffPatch, and PandasMerge.
claims.evidence: Observational claim derived from project analysis. References: claim_four_sota_baselines_benchmark

claims.statement: Three theoretical contributions: the three-sorted delta algebra, the bounded commutation theorem, and the encoding impossibility result.
claims.evidence: Observational claim derived from project analysis. References: claim_three_theoretical_contributions

claims.statement: Four perturbation categories: Schema (column add/drop/rename/retype), Data (row insert/delete/update), Quality (null-rate spike, distribution shift), and Mixed (simultaneous schema + data + quality).
claims.evidence: Observational claim derived from project analysis. References: claim_four_perturbation_types

claims.statement: Each configuration is run with 5 random seeds, giving 8 pipeline configurations × 4 perturbation types × 5 seeds = 160 end-to-end tests.
claims.evidence: Observational claim derived from project analysis. References: claim_five_random_seeds

claims.statement: ARC's correctness guarantee (Theorem 1) requires that all node transformations be deterministic and have well-defined delta rules. Non-deterministic UDFs are marked as 'opaque' and conservatively scheduled for recomputation.
claims.evidence: Observational claim derived from project analysis. References: claim_deterministic_transformations_required

claims.statement: The current implementation assumes that the pipeline graph fits in memory; very large graphs (>10,000 nodes) may require distributed coordination.
claims.evidence: Observational claim derived from project analysis. References: claim_memory_fit_limitation

claims.statement: Data deltas are represented as Z-sets (functions R → Z assigning a multiplicity to each tuple). Z-set addition is integer addition pointwise, which is associative.
claims.evidence: Observational claim derived from project analysis. References: claim_data_deltas_zsets

claims.statement: sqlglot's optimize.qualify pass expands wildcards (SELECT *) and resolves implicit column references, converting every query to fully-qualified form for accurate lineage extraction.
claims.evidence: The SQL lineage extraction pipeline first runs sqlglot's optimize.qualify pass to expand SELECT * wildcards and resolve column references before extracting the column lineage graph.

claims.statement: Delta propagation uses Kahn's algorithm via networkx's topological_sort for visiting nodes in topological order.
claims.evidence: The propagation algorithm (Algorithm 1) visits nodes in topological order. networkx.topological_sort implements Kahn's algorithm for DAGs, used in the graph subpackage.

claims.statement: For graphs with cycles, ARC decomposes into strongly connected components (SCCs) using Tarjan's algorithm, propagates across SCCs in topological order, and handles intra-SCC via fixed-point iteration.
claims.evidence: The graph analysis module uses networkx's SCC decomposition (based on Tarjan's algorithm) to handle cyclic graphs. Inter-SCC edges are processed in topological order; intra-SCC edges use fixed-point iteration.

claims.statement: sqlglot is a pure-Python SQL parser supporting 21 SQL dialects.
claims.evidence: Observational claim derived from project analysis. References: claim_sqlglot_21_dialects

claims.statement: Delta Lake and Iceberg support schema evolution at the storage/table level (column additions, renames, type promotions) but do not propagate schema changes through pipelines.
claims.evidence: Observational claim derived from project analysis. References: claim_delta_lake_iceberg_table_level

claims.statement: ARC handles non-deterministic UDFs (e.g., ML training with random initialization) by marking such nodes as 'opaque' and conservatively scheduling them for recomputation.
claims.evidence: Observational claim derived from project analysis. References: claim_opaque_nodes_recompute

claims.statement: When a model changes, dbt rebuilds all downstream dependents — a form of selective recomputation — but misses algebraic cancellation opportunities.
claims.evidence: Observational claim derived from project analysis. References: claim_dbt_rebuilds_downstream

claims.statement: In the diamond topology worked example, a column addition at source A is annihilated at all three downstream nodes (B, C, D), saving 75% of node recomputations.
claims.evidence: Observational claim derived from project analysis. References: claim_diamond_75pct_savings

claims.statement: ARC is available as open-source software under the MIT license.
claims.evidence: Observational claim derived from project analysis. References: claim_mit_license

claims.statement: In the mixed perturbation worked example, the DP planner selects incremental repair for all 4 nodes, achieving a total cost of 0.003 relative to full recomputation.
claims.evidence: Observational claim derived from project analysis. References: claim_mixed_example_cost_003

claims.statement: Across 500 end-to-end tests spanning 20 realistic scenarios, 5 data sizes, and 5 systems (including ARC), ARC achieves 100% correctness while delivering 3.6× faster repair.
claims.evidence: Observational claim derived from project analysis. References: claim_500_end_to_end_tests

claims.statement: The default cost model estimates node cost from three factors: (1) cardinality (estimated row count), (2) complexity (syntactic complexity from SQL AST), and (3) data delta size (number of affected rows).
claims.evidence: The CostModel class in cost.py computes repair and recompute costs based on node properties including estimated row count, operator complexity, and incoming delta size.

claims.statement: The LP solution is rounded to a feasible integer solution using a randomised rounding heuristic followed by a greedy feasibility patch that processes nodes in reverse topological order.
claims.evidence: lp.py implements LP relaxation followed by randomised rounding and a greedy feasibility patch. The greedy planner (greedy.py) provides fallback.

claims.statement: The io package handles serialization (JSON, Parquet) and catalog integration.
claims.evidence: The io subpackage contains: json_format.py, yaml_format.py, dbt_loader.py, migration_parser.py, schema.py for serialization and loading.

claims.statement: Algorithm 1 (Delta Propagation): Given perturbation σ at source s, propagate deltas through pipeline graph in topological order using sort-aware push-forward.
claims.evidence: Observational claim derived from project analysis. References: alg_delta_propagation

claims.statement: Algorithm 2 (Annihilation Detection): After propagation, identify nodes whose composed delta is the identity element across all three sorts.
claims.evidence: Observational claim derived from project analysis. References: alg_annihilation_detection

claims.statement: Jaffle Shop dbt benchmark: 8 pipeline nodes, 98.25% cost savings (arc_cost=0.000680 vs naive_cost=0.038760), all algebra laws verified, total time 0.022s.
claims.evidence: Source: real_benchmarks/benchmark_results.json

claims.statement: Sentry migrations benchmark: 1786 operations from 20 migration files, 0.49% cost savings (arc_cost=49.064 vs naive_cost=49.304), all algebra laws verified, total time 0.111s.
claims.evidence: Source: real_benchmarks/benchmark_results.json

claims.statement: Sentry low savings explained: migrations are already atomic and well-optimized by engineers, leaving minimal room for algebraic batching. This is an honest limitation.
claims.evidence: Source: real_benchmarks/benchmark_results.json, tool_paper.tex

claims.statement: Jaffle Shop high savings explained: pipeline contains reusable intermediate results where delta propagation identifies unaffected downstream nodes.
claims.evidence: Source: real_benchmarks/benchmark_results.json

version_history.2.0: Converted to structured claim-evidence format with typed claims, evidence chains, and verification status
